1: A thematic connection: 'After this I looked, and behold, a door standing open in heaven!' The open door recalls Christ's own promise in Revelation 3:8 ('I have set before you an open door') and his self-identification as the one who opens what no one shuts. The voice 'like a trumpet' that calls John up is the same Christ-voice of Revelation 1:10-11. John ascends in the Spirit to see 'what must take place after this' -- a phrase drawn from Daniel 2:28-29 (the revelation given to Daniel about end-time events). The throne room vision is Christ's invitation to see what he alone can show.
2: A thematic connection: the throne in heaven and 'one seated on the throne' -- the Father's transcendent majesty. The vision of the divine throne (Isaiah 6:1-3; Ezekiel 1:26-28; Daniel 7:9) is the necessary context for understanding what follows in chapter 5. The Father's sovereign throne is the seat from which the Lamb will receive the scroll. Everything in Revelation -- every judgment, every salvation -- flows from the authority of the one seated here. This is also the throne on which the Lamb and the Father will sit together (Revelation 3:21; Revelation 5:6-7; Revelation 22:1,3).
3: A thematic connection: the appearance of jasper and carnelian glory, with a rainbow of emerald around the throne. Ezekiel 1:28 describes 'the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the LORD' in rainbow-and-fire language, and Revelation 21:11 describes the New Jerusalem with jasper brilliance. The divine glory revealed in the throne room is the same glory the Lamb shares (Revelation 5:12-13) and that fills the new creation (Revelation 21:23: 'the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb'). The throne room is the source of the glory that ultimately transforms all things.
4: A thematic connection: twenty-four elders clothed in white with golden crowns surround the throne. The elders likely represent the full people of God -- twelve patriarchs of Israel and twelve apostles of the Lamb (cf. Revelation 21:12-14) -- the whole covenant community, OT and NT, gathered in the throne room. Their white garments are the gift of Christ (Revelation 3:5; Revelation 7:14: 'washed white in the blood of the Lamb'). Their crowns are the victor's crowns they will cast before the throne (Revelation 4:10) and before the Lamb (Revelation 5:8-14). They are there because of what Christ accomplished.
5: A thematic connection: 'From the throne came flashes of lightning, and rumblings and peals of thunder, and before the throne were burning seven torches of fire, which are the seven spirits of God.' The Sinai theophany (Exodus 19:16-19 -- lightning, thunder, the divine presence) is transported to the heavenly throne room. The seven torches of fire are identified as 'the seven spirits of God' -- the same sevenfold Spirit that Christ holds (Revelation 3:1) and that the Lamb has as his seven eyes (Revelation 5:6). The Spirit who fills the throne room is the Spirit of Christ, sent from the throne through the Lamb.
6: A thematic connection: 'Before the throne there was as it were a sea of glass, like crystal.' The sea in Hebrew cosmology represents chaos, death, and the forces opposing God's order. That the sea before the throne is glass -- still, transparent, conquered -- declares that God reigns over all chaos absolutely. The redeemed in 15:2 will 'stand beside the sea of glass' after their victory. In Revelation 21:1 'the sea was no more' -- it is completely overcome by Christ's triumph. The glassy sea frames the setting of the Lamb's worthiness that chapter 5 will display.
7: A thematic connection: the four living creatures -- like a lion, an ox, a man, and an eagle -- represent the fullness of created life worshiping before God (Ezekiel 1:5-14; Isaiah 6:2). Irenaeus connected these four faces to the four Gospel portraits of Christ (lion/Matthew-kingly, ox/Luke-priestly, man/John-incarnate, eagle/Mark-prophetic), reflecting the patristic instinct that the living creatures mediate between the Creator's throne-room glory and the new creation's redemption through Christ. The creatures lead the worship into which the Lamb is inserted in chapter 5.
8: A thematic connection: the four living creatures sing without ceasing, 'Holy, holy, holy, is the Lord God Almighty, who was and is and is to come!' The Trisagion of Isaiah 6:3 -- the worship that filled the temple in Isaiah's commissioning vision -- is now the unceasing worship of the heavenly throne room. John 12:41 states that Isaiah 'saw his glory and spoke of him' -- the 'him' being Christ. The seraphic worship of the holy God in Isaiah's vision was directed at the pre-incarnate Son. The throne-room Trisagion is thus continuous with the worship of Christ that chapter 5 makes explicit.
9: A thematic connection: 'whenever the living creatures give glory and honor and thanks to him who is seated on the throne, who lives forever and ever...' The worship of the one on the throne is the frame into which the Lamb's worship is inserted in Revelation 5:8-14. The same language -- 'glory and honor' -- appears in 5:12 as the sevenfold acclamation of the Lamb. The deliberate verbal parallelism shows that the Lamb receives the same quality and kind of worship as the Father, establishing his full divine status within the throne room's worship economy.
10: A thematic connection: 'the twenty-four elders fall down before him who is seated on the throne and worship him who lives forever and ever. They cast their crowns before the throne.' The elders' prostration before the Father in 4:10 is mirrored by their prostration before the Lamb in Revelation 5:8. The same redeemed community that worships the Father's sovereignty worships the Lamb's redemptive worthiness with the same act. The casting of crowns is the acknowledgment that every victory they have is received from God -- and in chapter 5, they recognize that it was received through the Lamb's sacrifice.
11: A thematic connection: 'Worthy are you, our Lord and God, to receive glory and honor and power, for you created all things, and by your will they existed and were created.' The first 'worthy' (axios) acclamation in Revelation is directed to the Creator. In 5:9 the exact word recurs: 'Worthy are you to take the scroll and to open its seals' -- directed to the Lamb. Creation-worthiness and redemption-worthiness are attributed by the same word to God and to Christ. The Creator who made all things by his will is the same Christ through whom all things were made (John 1:3; Colossians 1:16).
